name: Fotios
epithet: The One Who Prayed and Heard Nothing
bio: Fotios was seventeen when Sparta held the pass, old enough to fight and young enough that his captain pulled him back before the final stand — not cowardice, but arithmetic, someone needed to carry word home. He prayed through every hour of that final night, to Ares specifically, the god his city had built its entire identity around. He prayed for strength for the men in the pass. He prayed for a sign. He heard nothing.

That absence, that specific silence in exactly the place a lifetime of belief had told him something would answer, is the beginning of his actual story rather than the end of it. He spends years afterward not losing faith — faith in Ares is not something a Spartan loses easily — but working through what it means that a god can exist and still not answer, whether through distance, distraction, or something worse.

By Chapter 30, Fotios finally hears something, though what he hears is not the answer he spent years expecting. He is the mortal most changed by what Chronos's braided history does to the divine order — not because he fights in any of its major battles, but because he spends the entire story paying the kind of quiet, sustained attention that gods are not used to mortals sustaining. What he eventually understands changes how he prays, which changes what he is praying to, which changes everything.
